printer-a.why: Strongest: the line 'a kitchen that names everything on day one collects names it never uses, and loose names drift' is pure Chapter 0 voice. Weakest: 'Let the blanks choose and the words are not yours. You will not recognise them under pressure' is opaque on first read — the reader cannot tell who 'you' is under pressure or why.

printer-b.why: Strongest: the clearest structure of the four — numbered lists for the six things and two numbered failure modes make it scannable. Weakest: the voice is competent but flatter than Chapter 0; few sentences have the short punchy rhythm that characterises the standard, and 'Never make one kitchen's language the law' reads as a heading rather than a rule spoken to the reader.

printer-c.why: Strongest: the concrete contrast 'if dish means a unit of work, it cannot later mean a plate of food' is the sharpest single example across all four candidates. Weakest: the 'Steps' section (1-5 numbered procedure) breaks the book's voice — Chapter 0 never uses procedural numbered steps in the body, and this reads like a training manual rather than a conversation with the owner.

printer-d.why: Strongest: the 'done' example — one person thinks plated, the other thinks tasted-and-sent-back — is exactly the kind of concrete kitchen scenario Chapter 0 uses, and the sentence rhythm ('Not here. Not in a copy of this book. Not in a note you keep on your own machine.') matches the voice standard precisely. Weakest: slightly long in the 'words are then fixed' section, where two failure modes are explained in more words than Chapter 0 would use for the same idea.
